mappings.rationaleAI: The egg as the starting material for animal development, activated by fertilization or parthenogenesis (D.1), with shell, yolk stores, and extraembryonic membranes as embryonic adaptations (D.2.b).
Fruit formation from the ovary post-fertilization as part of seed dispersal in the independent dormant stage of plant development (C.3.b).
Flowers as the site of preparatory developmental events including pollination (C.2.b) and fertilization (C.2.c) in angiosperms.
Seeds containing embryos with nutrient-storage tissue, enabling dormancy and germination as part of early plant development (C.3), and seed dispersal mechanisms (C.3.b).
Irreversible cessation of biological functions as the endpoint of aging and decline (E), with coverage of the duration of life and causes of mortality (E.3).
Intrauterine gestation covering embryonic and fetal development (D.2), placental adaptations for maintaining the developing embryo (D.2.b), and organ formation through trimesters (D.3).
Childhood as a stage of postembryonic development involving progressive cognitive, physical, and social maturation (D.4).
Organisms defined by autonomous reproduction, growth, and metabolism, relevant to the nature and scope of biological development broadly (A).
Hormones as signaling molecules controlling and integrating development (B.4), including growth hormone, estrogen, cortisol, and plant hormones like auxin.
Biology as the overarching science of life encompasses developmental biology as a subdiscipline, relevant to the nature and scope of biological development (A).
Reproductive processes including gametogenesis and fertilization as preparatory events for plant development (C.2) and egg activation in animals (D.1).
Trees as perennial plants with woody trunks exemplify later sporophyte development including vascular tissue formation and secondary growth (C.4).
Lung organogenesis from foregut outpouching is an example of organ formation during animal development (D.3).
Embryonic brain division into forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain represents organ formation during animal development (D.3).
Bone formation through osteoblasts and the fusion from ~300 bones at birth to 206 in adults illustrates organ formation (D.3) and postembryonic development (D.4).
Evolutionary biology provides the framework for understanding the nature and scope of biological development (A), including ontogeny as distinguished from phylogeny.
Human life cycle from birth through maturity as an example of postembryonic development transforming the newborn into the adult (D.4).
Attainment of biological and social maturity as the endpoint of postembryonic development (D.4).
Puberty-driven physical changes including growth spurt and reproductive maturation as a stage in postembryonic development (D.4).
The origin of life from non-living chemistry relates to the broadest scope of biological development (A), covering the emergence of self-replicating systems and cell membranes.
